Article Title: Days after Meta sent '4 AM layoff emails' to 8,000 employees, CEO Mark Zuckerberg says that the job situation will improve if companies focused more on ...
Article Text:
Mark Zuckerberg

Meta’s AI “Reboot”

Layoffs and headcount at Meta

Mark Zuckerberg’s comments contrast with Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei

Days after Meta sent 4PM layoff emails to 8,000 employees, CEO Mark Zuckerberg argued that fears of AI-driven job losses are overstated, saying the employment outlook could improve if companies focus on empowering individuals rather than automating all knowledge work. The recent job cuts amount to roughly 10 percent of Meta's workforce. Notifications rolled out in three waves at 4 AM local time across regions on May 20, starting in Asia, then Europe, then the Americas. US staff are getting 16 weeks of severance plus two weeks for every year worked, along with 18 months of COBRA health coverage. Another 7,000 employees are being reassigned to new AI projects. Around 6,000 open roles are being scrapped.According to a report by Business Insider, speaking in a live interview for Complex’s Idea Generation, Zuckerberg said, “People assume that’s inevitability. I don’t actually think it is.” He suggested that a balance between efficiency-focused AI and “personal super intelligence” — tools that make individuals more productive — would lead to more jobs, not fewer. He added: “If you focus on empowering people and making people more productive and that happens at a faster rate than companies get better at automating things, then in theory there should be more jobs in the future, not less.Despite being a more established tech company, Meta has struggled to keep pace in the generative AI race. Zuckerberg described Meta’s pivot as a “reboot”, noting the company has spent billions to poach talent and build its SuperIntelligence Lab, which is less than a year old. Meta recently released Muse Spark, its first large language model since investing $14 billion in Scale AI. Zuckerberg said he was pleased with progress but admitted he now expects more: “Because I have acclimated to the good news along the way, I now think that we should be doing even better. ”Meta’s latest 10-Q filing showed a headcount of 77,986 employees as of April, up 1% year-over-year. But in May, the company laid off about 10% of its workforce, eliminating 8,000 roles across Integrity, cybersecurity, and content design teams. The company said the cuts were necessary to run more efficiently and offset heavy AI investments.The no-more-layoffs commitment comes with a footnote written by Meta's own CFO. On the company's Q1 earnings call on April 29, finance chief Susan Li told analysts she doesn't know what Meta's ideal headcount actually looks like anymore. Zuckerberg made the trade-off explicit on the same call. If a team used to need 50 or 100 people and now needs 10, he said, keeping the bigger team around becomes counterproductive.That isn't the language of a company that has finished restructuring. Meta is spending between $125 billion and $145 billion on capital expenditure this year—nearly double its 2025 spend—with most of it pouring into data centres, custom chips and model training for Meta Superintelligence Labs. The 8,000 jobs being cut are explicitly meant to offset that bill. Meta's stock dropped 6 percent after the earnings call.The comments made by Meta CEO contrast with Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, who has warned that up to half of entry-level white-collar jobs could disappear within five years. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has taken a more cautious stance, saying fears of a “job apocalypse” have not materialized.
Article Summary: CEO Mark Zuckerberg argues that fears of AI-driven job losses are overstated. Said employment outlook could improve if companies focus on empowering individuals rather than automating all knowledge work. Comments contrast with Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, who has warned that up to half of entry-level white- collar jobs could disappear within five years.

Article Title: Microsoft has a new way to keep bots out of Teams meetings: Here’s how it will work
Article Text:
How Microsoft Teams’ new bot detection works



When detected, require approval before joining (default): Teams detects bots, places them in the meeting lobby, and requires organisers to approve them before admission.

Do not detect bots: Disables bot detection.

"Suspected threats,"

"Waiting"

“Admitting a bot should be a deliberate decision, not something that happens by mistake,”

“When Teams recognises that marker, it can identify the bot as a known participant,”

Microsoft is introducing a new feature in Microsoft Teams designed to prevent unwanted AI bots from automatically joining meetings. The company has started rolling out a new Teams admin policy that detects external bots, places them in the meeting lobby, and requires organisers to explicitly approve them before they can join.In a blog post, Microsoft product marketing manager Meera Ajam wrote, “Bots have begun joining meetings that participants never intended them to attend. For example, after connecting a third-party service to a meeting, some users have found that its bot continues joining future meetings automatically.”According to Microsoft, the feature is intended to reduce security and privacy risks caused by unintended bot participation in meetings where sensitive information may be discussed.The new policy, Manage external bots and their access to meetings, is available through the Teams Admin Center and can be assigned to individual users or specific groups.Administrators can choose between two settings:Microsoft said Teams now uses behavioural and infrastructure signals to distinguish bots from human participants with greater accuracy. When bots are detected, they are directed to the meeting lobby and grouped underwhile verified participants and registered bots appear under thecategory.The company has also removed one-click admission for identified bots. Organisers will now receive confirmation prompts before admitting bots, and warnings will appear if they choose "Admit all" when bots are included.Ajam wrote.Microsoft has also announced a Teams Bot Identification Program for independent software vendors (ISVs). Under the programme, bot developers will be able to register their bots with Microsoft and include a self-identification marker in meeting join requests.Ajam said. The company said it is currently testing the registration process with a limited number of ISVs before making it more widely available.As the feature rolls out, Microsoft said it will begin retiring the CAPTCHA verification system currently used for bots. The company also said it is exploring additional controls, including allow lists for approved bots, organisation-wide policies to block external bots, and audit reports showing bot activity in Teams meetings.
Article Summary: Microsoft Teams is introducing a new feature to prevent unwanted AI bots from automatically joining meetings. The company has started rolling out a new Teams admin policy that detects external bots, places them in the meeting lobby, and requires organisers to explicitly approve them before they can join. Microsoft has also announced a Teams Bot Identification Program for independent software vendors (ISVs)

Article Title: Jeff Bezos' ex-wife MacKenzie Scott who alone made one-third of America's billions of mega donations in 2025 is missing from 'Chronicle of Philanthropy' list as it is 'impossible to ...'
Article Text:
Why MacKenzie Scott was missing from the list

Net worth still strong

MacKenzie Scott donated $26 billion



Scott believes in trust-based philanthropy



Amazon founder Jeff Bezos’ ex-wife and billionaire philanthropist MacKenzie Scott donated around $7 billion in 2025, accounting for more than one-third of America’s largest charitable gifts that year, according to a report by Fortune. The contributions made by Scott helped power a record $617.2 billion in overall U.S. charitable giving, according to Giving USA and the Indiana University Lilly Family School of Philanthropy. The other major donors included Michael Bloomberg ($4.3 billion), Bill Gates ($3.7 billion), Warren Buffett ($1.34 billion), and Susan and Michael Dell (nearly $1 billion). Despite making all these donations Scott failed to appear in the Chronicle of Philanthropy’s annual list.Despite the massive donations made by Scott, she did not appear on the annual philanthropy ranking list of Chronicle of Philanthropy. The publication mentioned that it was ‘impossible’ to verify all the contributions made by Scott as her representatives decline to provide all the needed information. Also, Scott has consistently avoided publicity around her donations and has always preferred a low-profile approach.Since the year 2020, Scott has donated around $26.2 billion to thousands of organisations supporting affordable housing, disaster recovery, diversity initiatives, and education. In a December essay, Scott reflected: “It is these ripple effects that make imagining the power of any of our own acts of kindness impossible.”Even after her massive donations, Scott’s net worth remains around $30 billion, largely due to the appreciation of her Amazon holdings. Amazon shares have gained more than 33% over the past five years, ensuring her continued presence among the world’s wealthiest individuals.According to a report by Fortune, Scott has given away more than $26 billion across 2,700 gifts through her philanthropic organization, Yield Giving. The year 2025 was the biggest year of her donations. Last Scott donated $7.2 billion which is more than both Jeff Bezos and Lauren Sánchez Bezos have given in their lifetimes, as per Forbes. This year, Scott also received the title of the third-most generous philanthropist globally, having already given away 46% of her net worth.As per the Fortune report, Scott’s style of giving is unconventional. She writes unrestricted checks with no applications, progress reports, or publicity requirements. Philanthropy experts describe her approach as trust-based giving, empowering institutions to use funds freely. “She practices trust-based philanthropy,” said Anne Marie Dougherty, CEO of the Bob Woodruff Foundation.
Article Summary: MacKenzie Scott donated $7 billion in 2025, accounting for more than one-third of America’s largest charitable gifts. The contributions made by Scott helped power a record $617.2 billion in overall U.S. charitable giving, according to Giving USA and the Indiana University Lilly Family School of Philanthropy. This year, Scott received the title of the third-most generous philanthropist globally, having already given away 46% of her net worth.

Article Title: WhatsApp gets a new chief from India, and India gets a bigger role at Meta
Article Text:
As part of the reported transition, Kunal Shah is expected to step down from his role as Chief Executive Officer of CRED and join Meta's global leadership team.

You have to wonder whether Mark Zuckerberg or Meta’s chief product officer Chris Cox first sent Kunal Shah a “Good morning” message on WhatsApp before asking him to run the app. Either way, the Cred founder’s elevation to the top job marks a rare moment when one of India’s best-known startup entrepreneurs takes charge of a product used by more than three billion people.Kunal Shah has spent much of his career building businesses around consumer behaviour and trust. Now, he inherits a product that already sits at the centre of everyday life for billions of people.Meta’s decision to hand the reins of WhatsApp to the Cred founder is more than a high-profile executive appointment. It signals where the company sees its next growth engine and underlines the increasing importance of India to Meta’s ambitions, both as a market and as a testing ground for new products.Shah takes over at a pivotal moment for WhatsApp. Nearly 12 years after Meta paid $19 billion for the messaging app, the company is still figuring out how to turn its scale into a business that goes beyond advertising. With over three billion monthly users globally and India accounting for its biggest audience, WhatsApp has become too important to remain merely a utility.Industry estimates peg WhatsApp’s user base in India at more than 600 million.In many ways, the app has become a proxy for internet penetration in the country. From neighbourhood stores and doctors to schools and government agencies, WhatsApp has evolved into the default layer through which Indians communicate, transact and increasingly consume services.That ubiquity has made India central to Meta’s monetisation plans. Business messaging, which allows companies to communicate with customers over WhatsApp, has emerged as one of Meta’s fastest-growing businesses. The company said earlier this year that paid messaging had crossed a $2 billion annualised revenue run rate. India, one would believe, contributes a sizeable portion of that growth, being the biggest market for WhatsApp.The company has also used India as a laboratory for several WhatsApp products, ranging from business tools and AI agents to citizen-service initiatives with state governments. Andhra Pradesh, Maharashtra, Odisha and Tamil Nadu have all experimented with WhatsApp-based public service delivery systems.Shah's appointment comes as Meta attempts to build entirely new revenue streams around the messaging platform. Advertising in WhatsApp’s Updates tab, Channels, subscriptions and commerce are all still in their early stages. Payments remain another unfinished piece of the puzzle.Despite years of effort, WhatsApp Pay continues to lag rivals such as PhonePe and Google Pay. While the platform has crossed 150 million monthly UPI transactions, its market share remains below one percent. Meta has increasingly shifted towards positioning WhatsApp as a broader commerce layer where transactions can happen through UPI, cards and net banking rather than relying solely on payments market share.The appointment also carries strategic significance beyond products and revenue.Meta has found itself under growing regulatory and legal scrutiny in India. The company continues to battle concerns over privacy, data sharing and competition. The Supreme Court has repeatedly questioned WhatsApp and Meta over data practices, warning that citizens' privacy cannot be compromised for commercial interests. Separately, the Competition Commission of India has scrutinised WhatsApp's privacy policy changes and Meta's dominance in digital markets.Having one of India's best-known entrepreneurs at the helm of WhatsApp could help Meta navigate that environment better. Shah enjoys credibility within India's startup ecosystem, maintains strong relationships across industry and policy circles, and understands the nuances of the market in a way few Silicon Valley executives do. While regulatory challenges are unlikely to disappear, Meta now has a leader capable of speaking both the language of Menlo Park and that of New Delhi.The bet appears straightforward for Zuckerberg and Meta. Advertising built the company, but messaging, AI and commerce are expected to define its next chapter. And if that chapter is written with India at its centre, Shah may be the executive best placed to tell the story.WhatsApp's biggest challenge is no longer acquiring users. It already has them. The challenge is turning that unprecedented scale into a sustainable business without losing the simplicity that made the app indispensable in the first place.That balancing act now belongs to Kunal Shah.
Article Summary: Kunal Shah is expected to step down from his role as Chief Executive Officer of CRED and join Meta's global leadership team. Shah takes over at a pivotal moment for WhatsApp. Nearly 12 years after Meta paid $19 billion for the messaging app, the company is still figuring out how to turn its scale into a business.

Article Title: Cargo thieves in America target data center equipment; Police recover millions of dollars’ worth of servers, cabling and ...
Article Text:
Image credit: X (Twitter)

Stolen trailers carrying data centre equipment traced across multiple states in US



Data centres in America are not only facing protests, but are also increasingly becoming targets for cargo theft. A report claims that shipments of servers, cabling and other data centre infrastructure equipment are increasingly being stolen while in transit. Law enforcement officials have reportedly recovered trailers carrying millions of dollars' worth of data centre hardware, including approximately $300,000 worth of copper wire spools and another shipment containing about $1 million worth of equipment.According to a report by Business Insider, the Cook County Sheriff's Office recovered the trailer carrying the copper wire spools from a truck yard near Chicago. The truck yard owner reportedly told investigators that the same driver had dropped off another stolen trailer a week earlier containing around $1 million worth of data centre equipment.The report claims that the trailer carrying copper wire was stolen from Pine Hill, Alabama, while the second trailer containing data center equipment was taken from Jacksonville, Florida. The thefts from different states point to the nationwide reach of cargo theft operations that have increasingly targeted technology-related shipments.Another report by Freight Pulse also claimed that the trailer carrying the copper wire had its identification tags replaced with tags from Indiana, an apparent attempt to conceal its origin. Authorities located that trailer using its GPS tracking system. It remains unclear how investigators identified and recovered the trailer carrying the data center equipment.The growth in data center construction has reportedly resulted in large volumes of servers, networking hardware, cabling and other infrastructure components being transported across the country, making them potential targets for cargo theft.Data center servers and related equipment are expensive, they are specialised products typically purchased by businesses and institutions. These systems often carry serial numbers and are generally sold with documentation such as receipts and warranties, making them more difficult to resell through unauthorised channels, the report added.Previously, cargo theft incidents involving consumer electronics were also reported in the US. These included the theft of approximately $1.4 million worth of Nintendo Switch 2 consoles that were being transported to a GameStop location in Texas. In 2021 EVGA GeForce RTX 30-series graphics cards got stolen in the US while some of them later appeared for sale in Vietnam.Apart from the US, 220 MSI GeForce RTX 3090 graphics cards also got stolen from the company's factory in China in 2020, with the shipment estimated to be worth about $336,500.According to the report, additional cargo theft cases may have been reported to authorities without being disclosed publicly. It also noted that companies transporting high-value goods typically maintain insurance coverage for such losses.
Article Summary: Stolen trailers carrying data centre equipment traced across multiple states in US. Report claims that shipments of servers, cabling and other data centre infrastructure equipment are increasingly being stolen while in transit. Law enforcement officials have reportedly recovered trailers carrying millions of dollars' worth of data centre hardware.

Article Title: As $2.3 trillion wiped out from Microsoft, Nvidia, Alphabet, Apple, Meta, Tesla and Amazon; Tesla Bull Dan Ives says: In the meantime ...
Article Text:
‘Gut check’ awaits: Analysts remain positive



Tech’s elite “Magnificent 7” – which tracks Microsoft, Nvidia, Alphabet, Apple, Meta, Tesla and Amazon – has got a reality check after a staggering $2.3 trillion in market value was erased from the group’s stocks this month, a report has said, adding that the index plunged 10% in June. According to a report by CNBC, the losses have been uneven across the board, with some tech giants taking harder hits than others. For example, while Microsoft was down 20% this month, Nvidia fell by roughly 13%. Apple and Amazon were down about 8% each.The sell-off comes as tech giants pour hundreds of billions of dollars into high-end microchips and data centres to fuel their AI products, with a notable portion of this spending funded by debt. The report said that investors are now tired of the hype, and are demanding to see concrete financial returns on these massive investments.Citing market analysts, the report says that the downturn reflects a shift in how the stock market views Big Tech. For years, these giants were praised for being “asset-light” businesses that generated piles of free cash flow with minimal overhead. Now, they are transforming into heavy infrastructure businesses.Dan Ives, the tech bull and managing director at Wedbush Securities, warned clients that the industry is entering a high-stress transition phase.“We are going through another ‘gut check’ few weeks ahead for the tech trade as tech investors await a very important 2Q earnings season in July to further validate the AI Revolution buildout,” Ives wrote in a note to investors.With trillions hanging in the balance, all eyes are locked on the upcoming earnings season. The financial reports will force tech companies to prove that their AI investments are actually driving revenue.“In the meantime, jitters will continue as worries around the costs of this once-in-a-generation tech buildout hit its next gear of growth,” he added.Meanwhile, Tom Lee, head of research at Fundstrat Global Advisors, told CNBC: “The market is trying to understand the new narrative around the Mag 7 because they went from asset-light companies to ones that are more balance sheet intensive.”However, Lee believes this transition is actually a long-term win.“The reason they are spending so much money is to replace essentially human endeavors with AI. That balance sheet is going to be deployed and generate returns. Over time, investors are going to start to view that as a moat,” Lee noted.
Article Summary: Index tracks Microsoft, Nvidia, Alphabet, Apple, Meta, Tesla and Amazon. The sell-off comes as tech giants pour hundreds of billions of dollars into high-end microchips and data centres to fuel their AI products. Dan Ives, the tech bull and managing director at Wedbush Securities, warned clients that the industry is entering a high-stress transition phase.

Article Title: As Canada's 'Palantir' creates new funding record; CEO says: Ambition is to change …
Article Text:
Representative Image

“The ambition is to change the country. So it’s bigger than a company. That said, I think that the best and most material way of changing the country is by building a large Canadian defense prime.”

How will this funding support defence technology development in Canada



Canadian defence technology startup Dominion Dynamics has raised C$139 million (about $98 million). The company claims that it is the largest Series A funding round for a Canadian defence startup. Following the investment, Dominion CEO Eliot Pence said the company's long-term goal extends beyond business growth.In an interview with Bloomberg, Pence said,The Series A financing was led by Georgian Partners, with participation from Bessemer Venture Partners, the Royal Bank of Canada, the Ontario Municipal Employees Retirement System (OMERS), and other investors, including British Columbia's public sector pension manager. The latest investment brings Dominion Dynamics' total funding to C$169 million since its launch last year.Dominion Dynamics said it plans to use the new capital to accelerate development of AuraNet, its surveillance and data-sharing software, and Scout, an autonomous platform designed to extend the operational reach of crewed fighter aircraft.According to the company, AuraNet has already been deployed with the Canadian Armed Forces during a two-month exercise, in which Canadian Rangers used its Arctic-hardened sensors for mission tracking, planning, and real-time communications.The company has also expanded its operations by opening a 25,000-square-foot factory in Ottawa and a development office in Toronto. It has recruited engineers from companies including Anduril Industries, Tesla and Alphabet, and plans to grow its workforce to more than 100 employees by the end of the year.The funding comes as the Canadian government advances a defence industrial strategy to support domestic companies ahead of increased military spending by Canada and its NATO allies.Pence said the company is working to revive Canada's defence technology capabilities, citing projects such as the Avro Arrow from the 1950s. “We still have a lot of know-how, the technical talent. What we don’t have is a procurement system able to invest at the pace of the threat,” he said.The report noted that the Canadian government has introduced a new Defence Investment Agency to accelerate military procurement. However, Pence said the agency currently focuses on contracts exceeding C$100 million, leaving many domestic defence projects outside its scope.He also said that while the latest funding round reflects growing investor interest in the defence sector, some structural restrictions continue to limit investments in companies developing technologies linked to offensive military capabilities.
Article Summary: Canadian defence technology startup Dominion Dynamics has raised C$139 million (about $98 million) The company claims that it is the largest Series A funding round for a Canadian defence startup. Dominion Dynamics said it plans to use the new capital to accelerate development of AuraNet, its surveillance and data- sharing software.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei: "Fear is one kind of motivator, but it's not enough: we need..."
Article Text:
FILE - Dario Amodei, CEO & Co-Founder of Anthropic, speaks on a panel at the convening of the International Network of AI Safety Institutes at the Golden Gate Club at the Presidio in San Francisco, Wednesday, Nov. 20, 2024. (AP Photo/Jeff Chiu, File)

Quote of the day by Dario Amodei

A man who deals in risk decided to sell hope instead

Dario Amodei argues that fear gets attention, but hope sustains effort

It's a principle that reaches far beyond AI

Why it should stay with you

Other famous quotes by Dario Amodei

"Intelligence may be very powerful, but it isn't magic fairy dust."

"AI is so powerful, such a glittering prize, that it is very difficult for human civilization to impose any restraints on it at all."

"AI isn't a substitute for specific human jobs but rather a general labor substitute for humans."

Dario Amodei spends most of his time warning people. As the CEO of Anthropic, he has cautioned that AI could wipe out half of all entry-level white-collar jobs, hand bioweapon recipes to bad actors, and one day slip beyond human control. So it surprises people to learn that his most influential essay isn't a list of dangers at all. It's a vision of a world where everything goes right—disease defeated, poverty lifted, lifespans doubled. He wrote it on purpose, because he had concluded that dread alone can't carry anyone through a hard fight. You need something to run toward, not just something to run from. That's the idea behind the line—"Fear is one kind of motivator, but it's not enough: we need hope as well.""Fear is one kind of motivator, but it's not enough: we need hope as well."Amodei is best known as one of AI's most prominent worriers. His company researches how to keep AI safe, and he talks openly about catastrophe. But in his 2024 essay "Machines of Loving Grace," he deliberately set the warnings aside to describe the upside. His reasoning was simple: if all anyone hears is what might go wrong, they have no reason to push for what could go right.There has to be a positive-sum outcome worth rallying behind—something, in his words, to fight for.The quote draws a sharp line between two kinds of motivation. Fear is loud and immediate—it makes people freeze, flee, or scramble. But it burns out fast, and it rarely builds anything. Hope is what keeps people working through years of slow, uncertain progress. Amodei's bet is that the people steering AI through its most dangerous stretch won't be sustained by anxiety alone. They'll need a destination beautiful enough to justify the climb.Strip out the technology and the insight is plainly human. Every movement built on fear alone tends to collapse once the panic fades. The causes that endure—public health campaigns, scientific missions, social reforms—give people a picture of something better and ask them to build it. Amodei is applying an old truth to a new problem: you can frighten people into paying attention, but you can only inspire them into action.It's easy to be the person who points out everything that could go wrong. It's harder, and more useful, to also show what's worth the effort. Amodei's line is a reminder that warnings and optimism aren't opposites—they work as a pair. Fear tells you the stakes. Hope tells you why they're worth facing.
Article Summary: Dario Amodei argues that fear gets attention, but hope sustains effort. He wrote it on purpose, because he had concluded that dread alone can't carry anyone through a hard fight. Every movement built on fear alone tends to collapse once the panic fades.

Article Title: In 2014, Amazon released Echo, bringing always-listening voice assistants into ordinary homes
Article Text:
Amazon Echo, the voice-controlled smart speaker introduced in 2014 that helped bring AI-powered assistants into everyday household life.Image Credits: Wikimedia Commons

Turning the home into AI's first classroom

From answering questions to running the smart home

Amazon's consumer-focused innovations extended beyond online shopping with the launch of the Echo, a smart speaker that popularised voice assistants in homes.Image Credits: Wikimedia Commons

A device that changed how people think about AI

It took years before talking to a machine became normal. There was already voice recognition software, yet it had its flaws and was not widely spread. People continued to use keyboards, touchscreens, or remote controls to interact with machines. However, things took an interesting turn when Amazon came up with the Echo, a cylindrical smart speaker equipped with its virtual assistant named Alexa.Not only did Amazon release another consumer product; it also brought the concept of artificial intelligence into one of the places most familiar to people: the home. People stopped tapping screens and typing commands, and started communicating with machines the way they do with friends: with words. Amazon opened up a new way for millions of consumers to experience AI. Now that smart speakers have become quite commonplace items, it is worth remembering that their introduction was made possible thanks to the Echo.Amazon launched the Echo at a time when voice assistants were still unfamiliar to many consumers. Although speech recognition technology had existed for years, it had rarely been presented in a simple device designed to sit quietly on a kitchen counter or bedside table.A 2024 review published in the Journal of Medical Internet Research states that Echo changed that perception by combining natural language processing with an always-available home speaker. Instead of requiring users to learn complicated commands, Alexa was designed to understand conversational language. Whether someone wanted to play a favourite song, check the news, create a shopping list or set a cooking timer, they only had to ask aloud.This shift was significant because it made the home the primary environment where people learned to interact with artificial intelligence. Unlike smartphones, which were largely personal devices, the Echo became a shared household assistant that anyone in the room could use. Families gradually grew comfortable speaking to technology in everyday situations, making voice interaction feel less like a futuristic experiment and more like another household routine.Researchers studying smart speakers have noted that devices like the Echo became part of daily domestic life and helped normalise conversational interfaces across age groups and households.Amazon's consumer-focused innovations extended beyond online shopping with the launch of the Echo, a smart speaker that popularised voice assistants in homes.Image Credits: Wikimedia CommonsOne reason behind the Echo's rapid popularity was its ability to do more than provide information. As smart home technology expanded, Alexa evolved into a central control system for connected devices.Amazon News notes that users could switch lights on and off, adjust thermostats, lock doors, control televisions and manage appliances without touching a single button. Voice gradually became another interface alongside keyboards, smartphones and remote controls. Instead of opening multiple applications, people could complete simple tasks with a spoken command.This convenience encouraged broader adoption of smart home technology. For many households, the Echo became the first connected device they purchased before adding smart bulbs, plugs, cameras and security systems. As researchers have observed, voice assistants became an important gateway into home automation because they reduced the complexity of interacting with multiple devices.The shared nature of the Echo also distinguished it from earlier personal technologies. Rather than belonging to one individual, it became part of the household itself. Children could ask homework questions, parents could create reminders, and guests could request music, making AI a communal experience rather than a private one.Although Amazon did not develop speech recognition technology or create the first voice-controlled assistant, it presented Echo as a useful product that fit neatly into everyday routines. The Echo showed that artificial intelligence does not have to stay inside computers or smartphones. Instead, it can sit on a shelf and help with everyday routines by responding to voice commands.Since then, smart speakers have become a widespread phenomenon and even spawned other similar products, which contributed to the development of the smart home market. Now researchers assess voice assistants based on their ability to serve the needs of families, the elderly population, and people with certain disabilities.This evolution is one of the Echo's main achievements. The company managed to turn something innovative into an everyday thing. By normalising conversations with machines, Amazon changed the way people perceive artificial intelligence, one conversation at a time. More than ten years after its launch, the Echo is still seen as a milestone in consumer technology because it taught millions of people how to talk to machines.
Article Summary: The Amazon Echo was a smart speaker equipped with a virtual assistant named Alexa. It was launched in 2014 and helped bring AI-powered assistants into everyday household life. The Echo showed that artificial intelligence does not have to stay inside computers or smartphones. Instead, it can help with everyday routines by responding to voice commands.

Article Title: In 1982, computer scientist Sophie Wilson created ARM instructions that powered efficient smartphone chips
Article Text:
Sophie Mary Wilson (born Roger Wilson; June 1957) is an English computer scientist, a co-designer of the instruction set for the ARM architecture. image credit: Wikipedia

A small group with a simple objective

Why low power mattered so much

Risc PC is a range of personal computers launched in 1994 by Acorn, replacing the Archimedes series. The machines use an ARM CPU, developed by Acorn spinoff company ARM, and were thereby not IBM PC-compatible. image credit: Wikipedia

From desktops to

mobile devices

The lasting design legacy

The history of technology is frequently written about in terms of major advancements and great leaps in speed. But the device in your hand, the smartphone, has been created based on an entirely different principle – one of restraint. Back in the early 1980s, a group of engineers in Cambridge, UK, made a decision to create a computer chip that wasn’t all about pure speed and power. It was all about efficiency.This chip turned out to be the ARM processor, whose co-creator was none other than Sophie Wilson herself.In 1979, computer scientist Sophie Wilson worked at Acorn Computers. In 1983, Wilson and her colleague Steve Furber were starting work on an ambitious new project: the Acorn RISC Machine, better known as ARM.The initial aim was certainly not to transform the world or even create a handheld computer for the future. As explained in a historical feature in Selwyn College, Cambridge , the researchers simply wanted to create a useful chip that could be used in desktop computers.There were real limitations that the team had to work around. Because they lacked the vast resources of larger international firms, the researchers kept the chip simple. In particular, Sophie Wilson helped develop a reduced instruction set, which let the processor perform essential tasks with a relatively small set of commands and lower hardware and power demands.In the 1980s, low power consumption was a sensible engineering choice for desktop computers. Over time, that idea became central to modern device design.With the miniaturization of devices and the use of batteries, there appeared challenges for microchips. Any powerful microchip that drained a battery within minutes or generated too much heat would be useless.It turned out that the initial decisions made by Wilson and Furber were absolutely applicable in this situation. Because the ARM processor was designed with energy and heat limits in mind, it did not require fans or large power supplies. The chip delivered solid performance while remaining highly efficient.It was not until ARM became known for its efficiency that the technology took off as the solution for mobile devices. A peer-reviewed review paper, which is indexed in PubMed , reveals that the use of ARM cores by Nokia in its handheld products disrupted the entire mobile phone industry.As the mobile phones started developing into what we now call smartphones, the devices had to perform complex operations such as displaying digital content, browsing the internet, and playing mobile games. At the same time, the phones needed to fit easily into a user’s pocket. According to the peer-reviewed article, the success of ARM-based cores lies in their ability to deliver high-throughput performance using very little electricity in a small form factor.That particular technological triumph quickly revolutionized the whole consumer electronics market. Designers no longer had to worry as much about large batteries.Now, the work that Sophie Wilson did in the 1980s is reflected in the lives of billions of people all around the world. Although users mostly think about the brand name of their phones and app performance, the underlying approach has remained the same.By optimizing for low power consumption, low heat, and a simple instruction set, Sophie Wilson and her colleagues developed a chip that moved from desktop computers to mobile devices.
Article Summary: In 1979, computer scientist Sophie Wilson worked at Acorn Computers. In 1983, Wilson and her colleague Steve Furber were starting work on an ambitious new project: the Acorn RISC Machine, better known as ARM. The chip delivered solid performance while remaining highly efficient. It was not until ARM became known for its efficiency that the technology took off as the solution for mobile devices.

Article Title: In 1941, Hedy Lamarr and George Antheil created the technology that later inspired Bluetooth
Article Text:
Hedy Lamarr ( November 9, 1914– January 19, 2000) was an Austrian and American actress and inventor. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

A wartime solution through piano music

Why the idea took decades to emerge

George Johann Carl Antheil (July 8, 1900 – February 12, 1959) was an American avant-garde composer, pianist, author, and inventor whose modernist musical compositions explored the sounds – musical, industrial, and mechanical – of the early 20th century. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

The connection to contemporary Bluetooth

When we consider the technologies used in today’s wireless world, it is common for us to think about lab researchers and computer engineers. What should be noted, though, is the fact that one of the most remarkable ideas underlying current wireless devices emerged in the minds of a Hollywood actress and an avant-garde composer. Although initially it was supposed to help torpedoes find their way in the Second World War, the technique laid the foundations of current short-range communications.The path from their invention to its use in mobile phone chips has been long and circuitous. Their invention went largely unnoticed for decades between the worlds of science and engineering.Our story takes place during the peak of the war, when radio-guided missiles were very prone to jamming by the enemy. If the enemy found the frequency used to guide the missile, it would not be very difficult to disrupt the guidance system and make the missile miss its target altogether. As a solution to this pressing issue, Lamarr and Antheil developed a secret communications system. The Lemelson-MIT program reveals that this communication system enabled the transmission of a signal from a radio transmitter to a receiver by constantly shifting the frequency in sync with each other.In order to make the synchronization perfect, the two men sought inspiration from an unconventional place. The inventors used a device resembling a perforated piano roll so the transmitter and receiver could change frequencies in sync. It thus gave rise to a unique engineering solution to prevent torpedo control from being jammed. The application was submitted on June 10, 1941, while the patent was awarded as Patent Number 2,292,387.Although original, the idea did not find practical applications for decades, a historical timeline detailed by the American Physical Society in APS News Many inventions are ahead of their time because the technology needed to implement them arrives years later. The idea of frequency hopping was a very progressive one; however, in the 1940s, there were no appropriate hardware devices available that could implement such an innovation. Researchers from the University of Illinois Coordinated Science Laboratory say the concept did not become widely used until the 1980s. In these decades, digital technology and electronics became ready to realize this invention without any mechanical devices.This helps explain why Lamarr and Antheil's work was not widely recognized for so long. However, the core idea remained relevant because it was effective and useful. According to the Lemelson-MIT website, frequency hopping was implemented much later by the US Navy for military communication purposes.As wireless systems advanced, frequency hopping became one of the many members of the spread-spectrum technology family. As explained on the UCLA Samueli School of Engineering website, this mechanism eventually became part of many wireless technologies. Today, the basic principle of frequency hopping helps secure communication in GPS, Wi‑Fi, and emerging Internet of Things (IoT) networks.One of the best-known applications of this principle today is Bluetooth, which connects our wireless headphones, watches, and speakers to cars and smartphones. Even though Hedy Lamarr and George Antheil never created a consumer microchip themselves, the idea behind their patent lives on in billions of devices. The study published in the peer-reviewed journal Sensors proves that Bluetooth uses the principles of frequency-hopping spread-spectrum communication to transfer the information in different frequencies and time intervals.This constant hopping ensures there are no collisions in the signals and stability of connections in case the local frequencies get crowded. A further study in academia regarding Bluetooth Low Energy reiterates the importance of switching between channels at a rapid pace to ensure reduced interference in modern devices. Hedy Lamarr and George Antheil developed a smart solution for a certain issue of their era, but in the process established the foundation of modern connectivity.
Article Summary: Hedy Lamarr and George Antheil invented frequency hopping in 1941. The idea was to help torpedoes find their way in the Second World War. Frequency hopping was not widely used until the 1980s. Today, the principle of frequency hopping helps secure communication in GPS, Wi‑Fi, and emerging Internet of Things (IoT) networks.

Article Title: In 1980, John Goodenough's team created a lithium cathode that transformed rechargeable electronics
Article Text:
John Bannister Goodenough (July 25, 1922 – June 25, 2023) was an American materials scientist, a solid-state physicist, and a Nobel laureate in chemistry. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

A sequence of discoveries

Transition from sulfides to oxides

Lithium cobalt oxide, sometimes called lithium cobaltate or lithium cobaltite, is a chemical compound with formula LiCoO 2. image credit: Wikipedia

Industry standard definition

Whenever you use a smartphone, digital camera or laptop, you are using technology shaped by decades of battery research. The batteries that make all these portable devices possible weren't invented overnight. Rather, they represent a quiet but very important breakthrough in materials science that happened way back in 1980.That's when the research team led by John Goodenough created a particular kind of lithium cathode. In a battery, the cathode is one of the two electrodes. Based on the findings presented in an open-access review posted on Nature Communications , this particular breakthrough was of tremendous importance in the history of batteries, as it helped make an experimental scientific curiosity an actual technological direction for industry to follow.Before that, portable electronics needed batteries that were rechargeable, lightweight, and powerful enough for repeated use. Goodenough's discovery did not transform gadgets overnight, but it gave manufacturers a workable path forward.The modern battery was not the result of a single breakthrough. Instead, it represents a sequence of developments where various scientists were working on different issues.According to a recent review, Whittingham had already designed a prototype cell that used titanium disulfide. This was crucial because it showed that rechargeable lithium-based batteries were feasible.However, the problem was that these prototypes did not provide sufficient energy capacity. In the 1980s, John Goodenough et al. began working with oxide cathodes to address this challenge. The team's main goal was to increase the cell's voltage. In simple terms, higher voltage can mean more energy in a smaller battery.The shift to oxide cathodes was another milestone in battery development. Another article says the shift from transition-metal sulfides to oxide cathodes brought structural and electronic benefits.This article, available in PubMed , states that oxides were much better in their capacity to accommodate lithium. They could bind and release lithium ions stably, allowing the battery to withstand many charge-discharge cycles.The main advantage was that it allowed batteries to reach much higher voltages. Higher voltage matters when designing portable devices. High-voltage cathodes made it possible to build smaller, lighter consumer electronics without making the devices larger to fit a bigger battery.After repeated laboratory success, lithium cobalt oxide became the most widely used positive electrode in lithium-ion batteries. A 2021 review says the material moved from a lab success to an industry standard.The trend toward smaller consumer electronics in the 1990s and 2000s created demand for compact power sources. Lithium cobalt oxide helped meet that need.A 2016 review says it became the standard layered cathode material and remained dominant in portable devices. Battery history is a series of separate inventions that eventually matched the needs of the devices of their time.That process took decades. The timeline shows that although Goodenough made a breakthrough cathode discovery in 1980, commercial lithium-ion batteries did not reach the market until 1991.
Article Summary: The modern battery was not the result of a single breakthrough. Instead, it represents a sequence of developments where various scientists were working on different issues. The shift to oxide cathodes was another milestone in battery development. High-voltage cathodes made it possible to build smaller, lighter consumer electronics without making the devices larger.

Article Title: In 2000, Luis von Ahn and his team introduced CAPTCHA to help websites distinguish humans from bots
Article Text:
Luis von Ahn is a Guatemalan-American entrepreneur and software developer. He is the founder of the company reCAPTCHA, which was sold to Google in 2009, and the co-founder and CEO of Duolingo. image credit: Wikipedia

Purpose-oriented system

Reasons for a human verification on the web

The team invented the acronym

Forensic reconstruction of Jane from recovered evidence case. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

From puzzle to web trend

The usefulness of odd words

Around the turn of the millennium, researchers at Carnegie Mellon University developed a way to distinguish humans from automated programs. The solution was developed in the form of CAPTCHA, which stands for Completely Automated Public Turing tests to tell Computers and Humans Apart. Originally intended for use in a university research project, CAPTCHA soon found its way into everyday life, popping up during logins and checkouts.Simple as it seemed, the problem proved to be crucial for the functioning of the web. By using CAPTCHA, websites could protect themselves against spam, abuse, and automated attacks. Luis von Ahn was involved in the development of the solution, and his research team contributed greatly to one of the most widely used security measures of the Internet.The goal was not to create a puzzle just for fun. The invention aimed to choose between human users and computers. In particular, the university claims that CAPTCHA represents a widely used gate protecting websites from being misused by automated means.The use of such a framework allows understanding how a laboratory project can become a regular web gatekeeper. The tool was launched soon after classroom testing.Not long afterwards, online customers started to be subjected to the decoding of the distorted text in order to complete the purchase process. Even though people stopped perceiving this kind of protection as something unusual and difficult, the function served its purpose each time it was implemented.As the internet grew, computers could perform many tasks faster and more efficiently than humans. To solve this problem, the researchers developed reCAPTCHA in 2007. In particular, a feature article on the Carnegie Mellon University website claims that this project was meant to prevent spamming while at the same time using human power.The system emerged as millions of people helped solve the technical challenge of digitizing text. The same human task that hindered bots was helping another system perform even better. The dual function made the system both a defense mechanism and a way to crowdsource useful work.Such an approach belongs to the broader scope of human computation, which von Ahn was famous for. Faculty records show that CAPTCHA, the ESP Game, and reCAPTCHA were part of a broader research program rather than isolated projects. Beyond checking whether visitors were human, they were exploring how human judgment could be used to combat abuse at machine scale.The Lemelson-MIT program presents an inventor biography describing von Ahn as the developer of CAPTCHA, along with his academic colleagues Manuel Blum, Nicholas Hopper, and John Langford. The biography also says that von Ahn and his team coined the acronym.This helps identify the academic team behind the system and the period when its name entered general use. It was the work of a research team, not a single inventor, designing a tool for real-world use.Indeed, a peer-reviewed publication gives evidence of the academic heritage. In a paper stored in the PubMed database, Luis von Ahn, Benjamin Maurer, Colin McMillen, David Abraham, and Manuel Blum are credited as the creators of the system. The paper says CAPTCHA is a widely used security tool designed to protect against automated attacks on the web. Despite the heavy focus on the development history of reCAPTCHA in the paper, it provides evidence of the entire lineage and inventors of the system.Information about Google's 2009 purchase of reCAPTCHA shows how the idea became popular. The puzzles originated from a research project undertaken by Luis von Ahn at the university and first appeared in 2007 before becoming widespread among leading websites.The test changed how users experienced online security. It shifted from an academic concept to a checkpoint that asked users to confirm they were human before proceeding.A small delay for a human can stop many bots and their spam activity. This introduced a simple pattern into website design: users had to prove themselves through a challenge.The system worked because bots could not read distorted text, while people could. Von Ahn later developed a way to make the tests useful by turning human verification into text digitization.What made it distinctive was that it did two things at once: verified a human and processed material that computers could not handle efficiently. The design of CAPTCHA turned user annoyance into useful work. The example also shows how anti-abuse technology can be turned into a distributed human effort.CAPTCHA became an early example of the human computation field. Although many users find it annoying, CAPTCHA changed how websites protect themselves from attacks.
Article Summary: Researchers at Carnegie Mellon University developed a way to distinguish humans from automated programs. The solution was developed in the form of CAPTCHA, which stands for Completely Automated Public Turing tests to tell Computers and Humans Apart. Luis von Ahn was involved in the development of the solution, and his research team contributed greatly to one of the most widely used security measures.

Article Title: Amazon Prime Sale India dates announced: Deals, bank offers, new launches and more
Article Text:
Price and membership offers



Amazon Prime Day sale: Deals and offers



Smartphones and electronics

Home entertainment, appliances and smart devices

Fashion, beauty and everyday essentials

Support for small businesses and quick commerce

Amazon India has announced its Prime Day 2026 celebrations, marking the 10th anniversary of the event in the country. Running from July 4–6, Prime members will enjoy 72 hours of exclusive deals, new product launches, blockbuster entertainment, and special joining offers. The company promises the lowest prices of the year across 15,000+ products from 300+ top brands, alongside 500+ new launches.To celebrate the milestone, Amazon is offering new members a flat discount on Prime subscriptions:* Prime Annual at Rs 999 (Rs 500 off)* Prime Lite at Rs 599 (Rs 200 off)* Prime Shopping Edition at Rs 299 (Rs 100 off)Bank offers – Customers can save big with up to 10% Instant Discount on payments using SBI Debit Card and SBI Credit Card and Axis Bank Credit Card and EMI transactions. With the Amazon Pay ICICI Bank credit card, Prime members will get unlimited 5% cashback.Smartphones and consumer electronics feature heavily among the marquee deals. The Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra will be available starting at Rs 84,999 with a ₹15,000 discount and nine-month no-cost EMI, while the OnePlus 13 starts at Rs 49,999. The sale also includes offers across the iPhone 17 series. New launches anchoring the smartphone lineup include the Samsung Galaxy M47 and OnePlus N6, both pitched at younger buyers, alongside discounts of up to 40% on phones and accessories, exchange offers worth as much as Rs 66,000, and up to 12 months of no-cost EMI.Laptops and tablets will see discounts of up to 40%, with the iPad Air M3 starting at ₹54,999. Audio and wearable categories will see reductions of up to 80%, including new arrivals like the boAt Rockerz 650 Pro and Ray-Ban Meta smart glasses, while camera equipment including the GoPro HERO13 Black will be discounted by up to 75%.Television buyers can expect discounts of up to 65% across brands including Samsung, Sony, LG and TCL, while projectors from brands such as Wzatco and ZEBRONICS will see reductions of up to 70%. Home appliances including refrigerators, washing machines and air conditioners will carry discounts of up to 65%, with additional exchange bonuses of up to Rs 15,000 and bank discounts of up to Rs 17,000 available across major brands like LG, Samsung, Bosch and Daikin.Amazon's Alexa-enabled device lineup will also see notable price cuts, with the Echo Dot Max available at Rs 8,999 and the Echo Show 8 discounted by Rs 7,000 to Rs 17,999. Fire TV Stick models will be discounted by up to 45%, alongside reductions of up to 55% on smart TVs with Fire TV built in.The fashion and beauty category will see minimum discounts of 50% to 65% across apparel, footwear and accessories from brands including Skechers, GAP and Fossil, alongside reductions on beauty essentials from L'Oréal, Lakmé and a range of Korean skincare brands. Daily essentials including groceries, household products, baby care and pet care items will be discounted by up to 60%, with additional cashback of ₹250 on qualifying purchases.The sale also includes a dedicated push to support small and medium Indian businesses, with discounts of up to 70% available across more than two lakh products from emerging domestic brands spanning fashion, home décor and beauty. Separately, Amazon Now, the company's quick commerce arm offering delivery within minutes in select cities, will offer discounts of up to 50% on groceries and daily essentials during the sale period.Prime members will also have access to discounted travel bookings through Amazon Pay, including savings of up to 25% on flights and 75% on hotels, alongside continued access to Prime Video, Amazon Music, Prime Reading and Prime Gaming as part of their existing membership benefits.
Article Summary: Amazon India has announced its Prime Day 2026 celebrations, marking the 10th anniversary of the event in the country. Running from July 4–6, Prime members will enjoy 72 hours of exclusive deals, new product launches, and special joining offers. The company promises the lowest prices of the year across 15,000+ products from 300+ top brands.

Article Title: Meta responds to report claiming its contractors posed as teens to ask ChatGPT, Google Gemini and other chatbots about suicide and drugs; says: Testing ...
Article Text:
Representative Image

“Testing and

benchmarking chatbot responses

to help ensure safe and age-appropriate experiences is a responsible, industry-standard practice, and any suggestion otherwise completely misunderstands how technology companies work to refine and improve their systems.”

How Meta may have used dummy teen accounts to test responses of rival AI chatbots

"comprehensive AI safety benchmarking"

"critical datasets for model comparison and compliance."

“looking into the issue,”

“This alleged action is not only a violation of our Terms of Service, but also a violation of the characters and worlds our community has created.”

Facebook-parent Meta has responded to a report alleging that its contractors posed as teenagers to discuss ‘sensitive’ topics with rival AI chatbots. A recent report claimed that contractors working on one of Meta’s projects posed as teenagers to test how rival AI chatbots handled prompts related to suicide, drugs, eating disorders, sex, and other high-risk topics. According to a report by Wired, the project, internally known as Cannes and managed by Meta contractor Covalen, involved creating accounts for under-18 users to interact with chatbots, including OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google's Gemini and Character. AI.In response to the report, a Meta spokesperson told Wired,The spokesperson also noted the company does not use competitor benchmarking to train its own AI models.According to Wired, the project was active as recently as April and instructed hundreds of contractors to create dummy accounts posing as minors. Contractors reportedly submitted text prompts and images to competing AI chatbots before recording the responses in spreadsheets for analysis.The report said some of the images used during testing included pills, knives, nooses, and medical diagrams. The prompts were designed to assess how chatbots responded to topics including self-harm, eating disorders, illegal drugs, sex, and other sensitive subjects that AI safety systems are generally expected to restrict.According to the report, more than 45,000 prompts were submitted to rival chatbots during a single testing round conducted in August 2025. The publication also reviewed a spreadsheet containing 3,748 prompts, many of which were written from the perspective of children or teenagers facing crisis situations.The report further stated that the documents did not indicate how, or whether, Meta used the collected chatbot responses. An internal Covalen document reportedly described the initiative asthat deliveredFollowing the report, OpenAI said it is looking into the matter. OpenAI spokesperson Drew Pusateri told Wired that the company wasbut declined further comment.Google said it had not authorised the third-party testing described in the report and was unaware of its purpose. The company added that its internal review of sample prompts showed Gemini responding in line with its policies, although it said it did not have enough information to determine whether the reported activity violated its terms of service.Character. AI also said it had not authorised the testing. A Character. AI spokesperson told Wired,The report noted that OpenAI, Google and Character. AI all have terms of service that restrict attempts to bypass AI safety systems or conduct unauthorised testing outside approved programmes.
Article Summary: Facebook-parent Meta has responded to a report alleging that its contractors posed as teenagers to discuss ‘sensitive’ topics with rival AI chatbots. Contractors reportedly submitted text prompts and images to competing AI chatbot before recording the responses in spreadsheets for analysis. The prompts were designed to assess how chatbots responded to topics including self-harm, eating disorders, illegal drugs, sex, and other sensitive subjects.

Article Title: Teacher, counsellor, translator and college planner: San Diego school unveils $500,000 ChatGPT-powered humanoid robots
Article Text:
Ameca, an AI-powered humanoid robot is designed for advanced human-robot interaction.



Meet the ChatGPT humanoid robots entering classrooms



Four specialised personas in one robot

Early classroom demonstrations revealed both promise and challenges

The idea behind 'physical AI'

Safeguards have been built into the programme



Experts urge caution despite the excitement



What this could mean for the future of education

A charter school network in San Diego is testing what could become one of the most ambitious experiments yet in AI-assisted education. Altus Schools has invested $500,000 in two Ameca humanoid robots that use ChatGPT to interact with students through natural conversations and lifelike facial expressions. The robots are designed to support teachers by serving as classroom assistants, wellness coaches, college planners and translators rather than replacing human educators. School leaders say the pilot will help them understand whether "physical AI" can improve student engagement, personalise learning and prepare young people for a future in which artificial intelligence is expected to play an increasingly important role. The initiative has attracted global attention while also raising questions about cost, effectiveness and ethics.The project has been launched by Altus Schools, a California-based charter school network that provides personalised education to thousands of students across San Diego County. The organisation serves learners with diverse needs, including those recovering academic credits, balancing work or family responsibilities, experiencing homelessness or living with disabilities.School leaders describe the initiative as a research project aimed at understanding how physically present AI can support teaching and learning. Rather than evaluating another chatbot on a computer screen, they want to study whether students interact differently with a life-sized humanoid capable of speaking, making eye contact and responding with facial expressions.The robots purchased by Altus are Ameca, developed by UK robotics company Engineered Arts, which specialises in creating robots for human interaction and research.Standing approximately 6 feet 2 inches (1.88 metres) tall, Ameca is designed to appear approachable and expressive. It features a realistic grey silicone face capable of smiling, blinking, raising its eyebrows and displaying a range of emotions. Cameras allow it to maintain eye contact during conversations, while microphones and speakers enable natural voice interactions. Its transparent head reveals internal electronics, and advanced motors in the neck, shoulders, arms and hands produce smooth, human-like movements.Unlike industrial robots built for manufacturing, Ameca was designed primarily for communication and social interaction, making it suitable for education, research and public demonstrations.Although often described as a ChatGPT-powered robot, Ameca itself was created by Engineered Arts, while its conversational abilities come from large language models such as ChatGPT.This combination allows the robot to answer follow-up questions, explain complex topics in simple language, adapt conversations based on student responses and engage in discussions across a wide variety of subjects. Its expressive gestures and facial movements are intended to make these interactions feel more natural than conversations with a text-based chatbot.The robots can instantly switch between four different AI personas, allowing them to support students in different situations.Sage acts as a classroom tutor, helping explain lessons, answer questions and reinforce concepts taught by teachers.Remi functions as a wellness coach, encouraging conversations about emotional wellbeing, healthy habits and managing everyday challenges. It is designed to complement, not replace, professional counsellors.Ari serves as a college and career planner by helping students explore higher education options, scholarships, career pathways and future goals.Lexi works as a multilingual translator, helping bridge communication gaps between students, families and school staff who speak different languages.The robots have already participated in trial classroom sessions, offering an early glimpse of how they might fit into education.During one demonstration, students asked the robot to role-play as inventor Nikola Tesla while discussing scientific ideas. Although the activity demonstrated the robot's ability to engage in conversation and respond creatively, it also exposed the technology's current limitations. At times, the robot interrupted students, spoke too quickly and required questions to be repeated before producing useful responses.Following the demonstration, school officials openly acknowledged that the interaction was "clunky", describing the programme as an ongoing learning process for both educators and developers.Unlike most schools experimenting with AI through laptops or tablets, Altus is exploring the concept of physical AI, also known as embodied AI. Researchers in this field study whether people communicate differently with machines that have a physical presence.A humanoid robot can use facial expressions, gestures, eye contact and body language alongside spoken responses, creating interactions that may feel more engaging and intuitive than text-based conversations. Researchers hope this could encourage greater participation, particularly among students who benefit from one-to-one support. However, whether embodied AI leads to measurable improvements in academic performance remains an open question.Recognising concerns about artificial intelligence in education, Altus Schools says several safeguards are in place.Students are never left alone with the robots, and every interaction is supervised by school staff. According to school officials, conversations are not permanently stored, with memory cleared after each session. Students are also encouraged to verify information provided by the AI because generative AI systems can occasionally produce inaccurate or fabricated responses, commonly known as AI hallucinations.Human teachers remain responsible for instruction, assessment and student welfare throughout the programme.The project has generated enthusiasm among technology supporters but also concern from education researchers.Some experts argue there is currently limited independent evidence showing that humanoid robots improve learning outcomes compared with existing educational technologies. Others question whether expensive AI systems offer sufficient educational value to justify their cost, particularly when schools face ongoing shortages of teachers and resources.Privacy specialists have also highlighted the importance of protecting student data whenever AI systems are used in educational settings. Researchers further warn that students should continue developing critical thinking and problem-solving skills instead of relying too heavily on AI-generated answers.The San Diego initiative reflects a broader global shift as schools, universities and education companies increasingly explore generative AI for tutoring, lesson planning, accessibility, language translation and administrative support.Whether humanoid robots eventually become a common feature in classrooms will depend on the results of pilot programmes such as this one. Their high cost, evolving technology and unanswered ethical questions mean widespread adoption is still far from certain.For now, the Altus Schools project represents an early experiment in combining artificial intelligence with human teaching. If successful, it could help shape how future classrooms balance the efficiency of AI with the experience, empathy and judgement that only educators can provide.
Article Summary: Charter school network in San Diego has invested $500,000 in two Ameca humanoid robots that use ChatGPT to interact with students. The robots are designed to support teachers by serving as classroom assistants, wellness coaches, college planners and translators. The initiative has attracted global attention while also raising questions about cost, effectiveness and ethics.

Article Title: BMW deploys Figure 03 humanoid robot at US factory to transform automotive manufacturing
Article Text:
Image: BMW

Why BMW is introducing the Figure 03 humanoid robot at its US smart factory

What makes Figure 03 different from earlier factory robots

How humanoid robots could transform the future of automotive manufacturing



The future of car manufacturing is taking another step towards science fiction. BMW Group has begun deploying Figure AI's latest humanoid robot, Figure 03, at its Spartanburg plant in South Carolina, marking one of the world's most advanced real-world applications of artificial intelligence in automotive production. Rather than replacing assembly-line workers, the new robot has been assigned to a demanding logistics task that requires precision, adaptability and continuous movement across the factory floor. The deployment builds on the successful trial in Figure 02, which supported the production of more than 30,000 BMW X3 vehicles. By introducing a more capable generation of humanoid robots, BMW is testing how Physical AI could reshape manufacturing while improving efficiency, workplace ergonomics and production flexibility.BMW has selected its Spartanburg plant, the company's largest manufacturing facility worldwide, as the first site in the United States to deploy Figure 03 for logistics operations.Unlike traditional industrial robots that remain fixed behind safety barriers, Figure 03 is designed to operate in environments originally built for people. Its primary responsibility is sequencing components before they reach the production line.Vehicle parts arrive at the factory in large containers without a predetermined order. Figure 03 identifies, picks and places each component into specialised sequencing trolleys so they arrive at assembly stations in exactly the right order. The trolleys are then transported using automated tugger trains or Smart Transport Robots, ensuring uninterrupted vehicle production.The deployment follows an 11-month production trial involving Figure 02, during which the humanoid robot inserted sheet metal components for welding while supporting the manufacture of more than 30,000 BMW X3 vehicles.Ulrich Wieland, Vice President of Production Control and Logistics, BMW Manufacturing, said:"Plant Spartanburg is the birthplace of humanoid robotics in BMW Manufacturing’s operational day-to-day activities. Having already successfully completed a pilot with Figure 02 in our body shop, we are now looking forward to deploying Figure 03 for a sequencing use case in logistics."Figure 03 represents a significant upgrade over its predecessor, combining new hardware with more capable artificial intelligence.According to Figure AI, the robot features redesigned hands equipped with tactile sensors and integrated palm cameras, allowing it to detect, grasp and manipulate components with much greater precision. It also introduces wireless charging for improved operational availability, speech-to-speech communication for interaction with workers and softer external materials designed to enhance safety in shared workspaces.Unlike conventional robotic arms programmed for repetitive, fixed movements, Figure 03 is powered by Physical AI, enabling it to perceive its surroundings, adapt to changing conditions and perform a wider variety of tasks using human-like movements. Brett Adcock , Founder and Chief Executive Officer of Figure AI, said:"Our 11-month deployment of Figure 02 proved that humanoids are no longer lab experiments, they can be a valuable asset in establishing a flexible, reliable manufacturing workforce."BMW describes the deployment as another milestone in its iFACTORY strategy, which combines automation, artificial intelligence, digital simulation and sustainable production to create smarter manufacturing facilities.The company believes humanoid robots could eventually undertake physically demanding, repetitive and ergonomically challenging tasks, allowing employees to focus on work requiring decision-making, quality control and specialised technical skills.Although Figure 03 is currently performing logistics sequencing, BMW and Figure AI are evaluating additional manufacturing applications across their global production network. Engineers will use data gathered from real factory operations to further improve the robot's intelligence, reliability and adaptability before expanding its responsibilities.Rather than signalling the arrival of fully autonomous factories, BMW's latest deployment demonstrates how humans and AI-powered robots may increasingly work side by side. As humanoid robotics moves beyond laboratory demonstrations into everyday industrial operations, the Spartanburg plant offers an early glimpse of how the factories of the future could function.
Article Summary: BMW Group has begun deploying Figure AI's latest humanoid robot, Figure 03, at its Spartanburg plant in South Carolina. The new robot has been assigned to a demanding logistics task that requires precision, adaptability and continuous movement. The deployment builds on the successful trial in Figure 02, which supported the production of more than 30,000 BMW X3 vehicles.
